questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:
Explicit : Implicit :: Express : _______

A. Impress  B. Repress  C. Compress  D. Suppress
questions.to_find: word that completes the analogy Express : _______ in the same way as Explicit : Implicit

understand.plain: The analogy Explicit : Implicit uses a prefix pattern — 'im-' negates or reverses 'ex-'. We need the word that relates to 'Express' in the same way 'Implicit' relates to 'Explicit'. The prefix 'im-' on 'Explicit' gives 'Implicit' (hidden, not stated). For 'Express', adding 're-' gives 'Repress' (to hold back, suppress expression). The key is: Implicit is the opposite / negation of Explicit; Repress is the opposite / negation of Express.

keywords.explain: Clearly and directly stated; leaving nothing implied
keywords.example: An explicit instruction leaves no room for ambiguity.

keywords.explain: Implied or understood without being directly expressed; the opposite of explicit
keywords.example: An implicit agreement is one understood without being written down.

keywords.explain: To put thoughts or feelings into words or actions; to make known openly
keywords.example: She expressed her happiness through a smile.

keywords.explain: To hold back or suppress; to prevent from being expressed or acted upon
keywords.example: He repressed his anger and stayed calm.

understand.visual_alt: Word analogy showing prefix-based opposition between Explicit/Implicit and Express/Repress.

given.aim: Find the word that mirrors the relationship Explicit : Implicit when applied to Express

terms.meaning: Antonym pair where 'im-' reverses the meaning of 'ex-'; Implicit = not openly stated
terms.example: Explicit rules vs. implicit expectations in a workplace
terms.connects: Gives the template relationship: open/stated vs. hidden/suppressed

terms.meaning: To clearly state or convey a feeling or idea
terms.example: Express your opinion freely
terms.connects: The source word that needs its semantic opposite

terms.meaning: To hold back or suppress; to stop something from being expressed
terms.example: Repress emotions so they do not show
terms.connects: Mirrors the Explicit-to-Implicit negation for Express

given.plan: Identify the relationship type (antonym via prefix change), then apply the same change to 'Express' and match with the given options.

steps.title: Identify the relationship in the given pair
steps.apply: Explicit (openly stated) and Implicit (not stated, implied) are antonyms. The prefix 'im-' on 'Explicit' creates its opposite 'Implicit'.
steps.note: The core relationship is semantic opposition — one word directly negates/reverses the other.

steps.title: Apply the same pattern to 'Express'
steps.apply: Express means to openly state or convey. We need its opposite — a word meaning to hold back or suppress expression. 'Repress' means exactly that: to suppress or hold back.
steps.note: Among the options: Impress (to make an impact), Repress (to suppress), Compress (to compact), Suppress (to put down). Repress most closely mirrors the Explicit-Implicit relationship.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply: Impress means to affect strongly. Compress means to press together. Suppress also means to hold back but does not carry the specific semantic mirroring of 're-' as the reverse/undoing of expression. Repress specifically means to prevent from being expressed.
steps.note: Repress is the best fit as it is the true semantic opposite of Express in the context of this analogy.

questions.formula_note: Word analogy questions rely on semantic reasoning, not mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
Nostalgia is to anticipation as ____________ is to _____________
Which one of the following options maintains a similar logical relation in the above sentence?

A. Present, past  B. Future, past  C. Past, future  D. Future, present
questions.to_find: pair of words that maintains the same logical relationship as Nostalgia is to Anticipation

understand.plain: Nostalgia is the emotion of longing for the past — it is anchored in what has already happened. Anticipation is the emotion of looking forward to something that has not yet happened — it is anchored in the future. The relationship is: Nostalgia relates to the Past, and Anticipation relates to the Future. So we need a pair (X, Y) where X corresponds to the past and Y corresponds to the future.

keywords.explain: A sentimental longing or wistful affection for the past
keywords.example: Hearing an old song can trigger nostalgia for childhood.

keywords.explain: The feeling of excitement or expectation about something that will happen in the future
keywords.example: The anticipation before a birthday party is exciting.

keywords.explain: Both words describe emotional states tied to time — Nostalgia to the past, Anticipation to the future
keywords.example: Nostalgia looks backward; Anticipation looks forward.

understand.visual_alt: Time axis with Nostalgia pointing left (past) and Anticipation pointing right (future).

given.aim: Find a word pair (X, Y) such that X is associated with past and Y is associated with future, mirroring Nostalgia:Anticipation

terms.meaning: Emotion directed at the past; longing for what was
terms.example: Old photographs evoke nostalgia
terms.connects: Maps to 'Past' — first slot in the answer

terms.meaning: Emotion directed at the future; looking forward to what will be
terms.example: Anticipating a vacation creates excitement
terms.connects: Maps to 'Future' — second slot in the answer

terms.meaning: The correct pair that mirrors the directional emotion relationship
terms.example: Nostalgia -> Past, Anticipation -> Future
terms.connects: Confirms the answer is C: Past, future

given.plan: Determine what time period each emotion (Nostalgia, Anticipation) is associated with, then select the option that places the correct time words in the same order.

steps.title: Analyze the anchor relationship
steps.apply: Nostalgia is associated with the Past. Anticipation is associated with the Future. So the template is: [emotion for past] is to [emotion for future] as [past] is to [future].
steps.note: The blank pair must preserve the order: first = past, second = future.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Option A (Present, past) — wrong order and uses 'present'. Option B (Future, past) — reversed order. Option C (Past, future) — correct order matching the analogy. Option D (Future, present) — wrong words.
steps.note: Only option C maintains the exact ordering: Nostalgia(past) : Anticipation(future) :: Past : Future.

questions.formula_note: This analogy is solved by mapping each emotion to its associated time period, not by mathematical reasoning.

questions.question:
Courts : _________ :: Parliament : Legislature
(By word meaning)

A. Judiciary  B. Executive  C. Governmental  D. Legal
questions.to_find: word that relates to Courts the same way Legislature relates to Parliament

understand.plain: Parliament is the legislative body of government and its functional name (by role) is 'Legislature'. Similarly, Courts are the judicial body of government and their functional name (by role) is 'Judiciary'. The analogy asks: what is to Courts as Legislature is to Parliament? The answer is Judiciary, since the Judiciary is the branch of government that operates through courts.

keywords.explain: The supreme legislative body in many democratic nations, responsible for making laws
keywords.example: India's Parliament consists of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.

keywords.explain: The branch of government (or its function) that creates and passes laws; synonymous with Parliament in this context
keywords.example: The Legislature debated the new bill for three days.

keywords.explain: The branch of government that interprets and applies laws through courts
keywords.example: The Supreme Court is the apex of the Judiciary.

understand.visual_alt: Three branches of government: Legislature (Parliament), Executive, Judiciary (Courts).

given.aim: Identify the functional/systemic name for Courts, mirroring how Legislature is the name for Parliament

terms.meaning: The elected body responsible for legislation
terms.example: Members of Parliament debate and pass laws
terms.connects: Is to Legislature as Courts is to ___

terms.meaning: The formal term for the law-making branch of government
terms.example: The Indian Legislature has two houses
terms.connects: The functional/systemic label for Parliament

terms.meaning: Institutions where legal disputes are adjudicated by judges
terms.example: The District Court heard the civil case
terms.connects: Belongs to the Judiciary branch, just as Parliament belongs to the Legislature

given.plan: Identify what branch of government Courts belong to (Judiciary), the same way Parliament belongs to the Legislature.

steps.title: Map Parliament to its branch name
steps.apply: Parliament is the body; Legislature is the branch name. Parliament : Legislature = institution : branch.
steps.note: The relationship is an institution mapped to its branch of government.

steps.title: Apply the same mapping to Courts
steps.apply: Courts are the institutions of the Judiciary branch. So Courts : Judiciary mirrors Parliament : Legislature exactly.
steps.note: Executive and Legal are not the branch name for courts; Governmental is too generic.

questions.formula_note: This question tests knowledge of governmental branch terminology, not mathematical formulas.

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:
Do : Undo :: Trust : ______

A. Entrust  B. Intrust  C. Distrust  D. Untrust
questions.to_find: word that completes the analogy Trust : _______ in the same way as Do : Undo

understand.plain: In the pair Do : Undo, the prefix 'un-' reverses or negates the action — Undo means to reverse what was Done. We need a word that similarly reverses or negates Trust. 'Distrust' means to lack trust or regard with suspicion — it is the negation of Trust. While 'Untrust' seems logical by the same prefix pattern, it is not a standard English word. 'Distrust' is the correct established antonym.

keywords.explain: Undo reverses the action of Do — prefix 'un-' creates the reversal/negation
keywords.example: You can undo a typed word with Ctrl+Z.

keywords.explain: To have confidence or belief in the reliability, truth, or ability of something
keywords.example: I trust my best friend completely.

keywords.explain: To regard without trust or confidence; the negation of trust
keywords.example: Repeated lies lead to distrust.

understand.visual_alt: Word analogy showing negation pattern: Do/Undo and Trust/Distrust.

given.aim: Find the standard English word that is the reversal/negation of Trust, mirroring how Undo negates Do

terms.meaning: Undo = reverse of Do; the 'un-' prefix signals reversal
terms.example: Undo a knot by reversing the tying motion
terms.connects: Template relationship: action vs. its reversal

terms.meaning: Belief in honesty, reliability, or ability of another
terms.example: Trust is built over time through consistent actions
terms.connects: The source word requiring a negation/reversal

terms.meaning: Lack of trust; suspicion; negation of trust
terms.example: His secretive behavior caused distrust among colleagues
terms.connects: The standard antonym of Trust in English, playing the Undo role

given.plan: Identify the established English antonym of Trust that captures the same reversal meaning as Undo does for Do.

steps.title: Analyze the Do:Undo relationship
steps.apply: Undo = reverse/negate Do. The relationship is action : reversal-of-action.
steps.note: 'Un-' signals reversal, but the answer must be a real, standard English word.

steps.title: Find the established antonym of Trust
steps.apply: Entrust means to give responsibility to someone — not an antonym. Intrust is an archaic spelling of Entrust. Distrust means to regard with suspicion; it is the established opposite of Trust. Untrust is not a standard English word.
steps.note: Distrust is the only option that is both a real English word and the semantic opposite of Trust.

questions.formula_note: Analogy completion requires identifying the correct semantic relationship and applying it to find the standard vocabulary match.

questions.question:
Oasis is to sand as island is to _____________
Which one of the following options maintains a similar logical relation in the above sentence?

A. Stone  B. Land  C. Water  D. Mountain
questions.to_find: word that completes the analogy island is to _______ in the same way as oasis is to sand

understand.plain: An oasis is a fertile area surrounded by sand (desert). An island is a piece of land surrounded by water. In both cases, the second word is the medium or surrounding environment in which the first word (oasis/island) exists as an enclosed feature. The relationship is: oasis is a feature surrounded by sand; island is a feature surrounded by water.

keywords.explain: A fertile spot in a desert where water is found, surrounded by sand
keywords.example: Travelers rested at the oasis in the Sahara Desert.

keywords.explain: The surrounding medium of a desert; what encloses the oasis
keywords.example: The oasis was a green patch amid miles of sand.

keywords.explain: A piece of land surrounded entirely by water
keywords.example: Sri Lanka is an island nation surrounded by the Indian Ocean.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 160' width='400' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='160' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='30' width='170' height='100' fill='#e8d8a0' rx='8'/><text x='95' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Oasis in Sand</text><ellipse cx='95' cy='80' rx='35' ry='25' fill='#6abf6a'/><text x='95' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Oasis</text><text x='40' y='90' class='sub mut'>Sand</text><text x='130' y='110' class='sub mut'>Sand</text><rect x='220' y='30' width='170' height='100' fill='#6ab4e8' rx='8'/><text x='305' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Island in Water</text><ellipse cx='305' cy='80' rx='35' ry='25' fill='#8a8a40'/><text x='305' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Island</text><text x='240' y='90' class='sub mut'>Water</text><text x='340' y='110' class='sub mut'>Water</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two diagrams: left shows an oasis (green) surrounded by sand (tan); right shows an island (brown) surrounded by water (blue).

given.aim: Identify the surrounding medium for an island, mirroring how sand surrounds an oasis

terms.meaning: A feature (fertile area) enclosed within a desert
terms.example: The oasis had palm trees and a fresh spring
terms.connects: The isolated feature; sand is the surrounding medium

terms.meaning: The surrounding environment that encloses the oasis on all sides
terms.example: The Sahara is vast sand with occasional oases
terms.connects: Answers the second slot in the analogy for oasis

terms.meaning: A land mass surrounded entirely by a body of water
terms.example: Madagascar is the world's fourth largest island
terms.connects: Parallels oasis; its surrounding medium is water

given.plan: Recognize that both oasis and island are features completely enclosed by a different medium (sand and water respectively); identify that medium for island.

steps.title: Identify the structural relationship
steps.apply: An oasis is a feature completely surrounded by sand. The relationship is [feature] : [surrounding medium].
steps.note: The key insight is that sand is what surrounds and defines the oasis.

steps.title: Apply to island
steps.apply: An island is a feature (land) completely surrounded by water. So island : water mirrors oasis : sand perfectly.
steps.note: Stone, land, and mountain do not surround an island — water does.

questions.formula_note: Spatial analogy — the relationship is [enclosed feature] : [surrounding medium] for both pairs.

questions.question:
Permit : ________ :: Enforce : Relax
(By word meaning)

A. Allow  B. Forbid  C. License  D. Reinforce
questions.to_find: word that relates to Permit the same way Relax relates to Enforce

understand.plain: In the pair Enforce : Relax, the two words are antonyms — Enforce means to compel compliance (strict application), while Relax means to make less strict (ease off). We need the antonym of Permit. Permit means to allow or give permission; its antonym is Forbid (to prohibit or not allow). So the analogy is: Permit : Forbid :: Enforce : Relax, where both pairs are antonym pairs.

keywords.explain: To compel observance of a law or rule; to apply strictly
keywords.example: The police enforce traffic laws.

keywords.explain: To make (a rule or restriction) less strict; to ease
keywords.example: The government relaxed the lockdown restrictions.

keywords.explain: To allow; to give permission for something to happen
keywords.example: The school permits students to use the library after hours.

keywords.explain: To prohibit; to refuse permission; the antonym of permit
keywords.example: The management forbids smoking on the premises.

understand.visual_alt: Antonym pairs: Enforce vs Relax and Permit vs Forbid, both showing strict/lenient contrasts.

given.aim: Find the antonym of Permit that mirrors the Enforce:Relax antonym relationship

terms.meaning: Antonym pair — Enforce = apply strictly; Relax = ease up; opposite directions of strictness
terms.example: Enforce the rules vs. relax the rules
terms.connects: Template: the second word is the antonym of the first

terms.meaning: To grant permission; to allow
terms.example: The permit allows construction in that zone
terms.connects: The source word needing its antonym

terms.meaning: To prohibit; to deny permission; the exact opposite of Permit
terms.example: The law forbids driving under the influence
terms.connects: The antonym of Permit, completing the analogy

given.plan: Recognize Enforce:Relax as antonyms, then find the antonym of Permit from the options.

steps.title: Establish the relationship type
steps.apply: Enforce and Relax are antonyms (strict application vs. easing). So Permit and its pair must also be antonyms.
steps.note: The format X : Y :: Enforce : Relax requires Y to be the antonym of X (Permit).

steps.title: Find the antonym of Permit
steps.apply: Allow is a synonym of Permit — not its antonym. License is also similar to Permit. Reinforce means to strengthen — unrelated. Forbid means to prohibit, which is the direct antonym of Permit.
steps.note: Forbid is the only option that is the true antonym of Permit.

questions.formula_note: The analogy type is antonym pairs — both Enforce:Relax and Permit:Forbid are antonym relationships.

questions.question:
Disagree : Protest :: Agree : __________
(By word meaning)

A. Refuse  B. Protest  C. Recommend  D. Refute
questions.to_find: word that relates to Agree the same way Protest relates to Disagree

understand.plain: When you Disagree with something, a natural action is to Protest (to express your opposition actively). Similarly, when you Agree with something, a natural corresponding action is to Recommend (to express your support or endorsement actively). The relationship is: Disagree leads to the action of Protest (negative vocal expression); Agree leads to the action of Recommend (positive vocal expression of support).

keywords.explain: To hold a different opinion; to not concur with someone or something
keywords.example: She disagreed with the policy and said so.

keywords.explain: To express strong objection or disapproval, often publicly; the vocal action of disagreement
keywords.example: Citizens protested against the unfair law.

keywords.explain: To have the same opinion; to concur with someone or something
keywords.example: Both experts agreed on the diagnosis.

keywords.explain: To put forward something with approval; to endorse; the positive vocal action corresponding to agreement
keywords.example: She recommended the book highly to her students.

understand.visual_alt: Analogy showing Disagree leading to Protest (negative) and Agree leading to Recommend (positive).

given.aim: Find the positive action that follows from Agree, mirroring how Protest follows from Disagree

terms.meaning: Disagree = negative stance; Protest = vocal/active expression of that negative stance
terms.example: If you disagree with a policy, you protest it
terms.connects: Template: stance : active expression of that stance

terms.meaning: Positive stance; concurrence with an idea or proposal
terms.example: Agree with a friend's suggestion
terms.connects: Source word needing its active positive expression

terms.meaning: To endorse or advocate for something; active positive expression of agreement
terms.example: Recommend a restaurant you enjoyed to others
terms.connects: The active positive expression that mirrors Protest for the Agree side

given.plan: Identify that Protest is the active expression of Disagree, then find the active expression of Agree.

steps.title: Understand the Disagree:Protest relationship
steps.apply: Disagree = holding a negative opinion. Protest = actively voicing or acting on that negative opinion. The relationship is: mental stance -> active expression of that stance.
steps.note: Protest is the active/vocal manifestation of disagreement.

steps.title: Find the parallel for Agree
steps.apply: Agree = holding a positive opinion. The parallel active expression would be: advocating for it or endorsing it. Among options: Refuse = to decline (opposite of agree). Protest = expression of disagreement (wrong direction). Recommend = to endorse, support, speak in favor of. Refute = to disprove (involves disagreement).
steps.note: Recommend is the positive active expression that mirrors Protest as the negative active expression.

questions.formula_note: The analogy type is [stance] : [active expression of that stance]; Protest expresses Disagreement, and Recommend expresses Agreement.
